persons.arguments_against: GREGG is a common surname (8 officials, 3 bios) and the bio gives only two initials B. L., so granularity is limited and a relative could in principle share them. This fails because the stint reconstructs the bio's entire dated career as a chain of unique senior legal posts, in lockstep. Bio: 'clk. to atty.-gen., 1930; leg. dftsmn., 1938; dep. registr.-gen. ..., 1938; registr.-gen., and registr. of titles, Fiji, 1950-63.' Stint: Clerk to Attorney-General from 1932, Deputy Registrar-General / Deputy Registrar of Supreme Court from 1939, then 'Registrar-General and Registrar of Titles' 1953-63 — matching verbatim. There are 9 co-occurring editions on the senior post. A relative would not replicate this exact succession of one-of-a-kind offices with matching dates.

evidence_cited.fact: clk. to atty.-gen., 1930; leg. dftsmn., 1938; dep. registr.-gen. and dep. registr., sup. ct., 1938; registr.-gen., and registr. of titles, Fiji, 1950-63

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second B. L. Gregg holding the Fiji registrar-general line, splitting this continuous career between two men.

notes: Single continuous Fiji legal/registry career matching the bio step by step with 9 co-occurring editions; the unique title 'Registrar-General and Registrar of Titles' is decisive despite the common surname and two-initial bio.
